content:
First Outbound Squad off-site in the books! I couldn't be more excited about what we're growing with these world-class humans Amanda Long Clara Johnson Aaron Milner 📈

This crew...holy sh*t. Each of them are top reps, world-class leaders, and deliver amazing results for our clients. 

But most importantly, they're amazing people. Leadership is a privilege. And I feel privileged to be their leader.

Here's our vision for Outbound Squad:

1) Build THE methodology for outbound PipeGen. Sellers have MEDDICC, Sandler, or Challenger. But outbound happens on accident with little structure or guidance besides a script and an email sequence. We've built the first and only outbound methodology, and we want to continue making a huge impact with sales orgs like Shopify, Zoom, Rippling, Gong, and more. Every seller. Every org. Every enablement teams needs an outbound pipegen methodology that delivers results.

2) Build the best outbound thought leadership in the game. Outbound is due for a great book. Not just on cold calling. Or how to be more motivated to outbound. A tactical guide for the organization to scale outbound pipegen. The tactics, the system, reinforcement, everything. A book is on the docket for next year. We want to conduct a formal study on how the best sales orgs on the planet build pipeline. Think Challenger, but for outbound pipegen.

3) Build a family. We're curated a group of badasses who are highly talented, give a sh*t, who want to be PUSHED to make a big impact...all while experiencing more work-life balance than any other role they’ve been in (we do 4-day workweeks). We want to give world-class sales & enablement leaders a career that helps them escape the grind.

I decided last year that it's no longer the Jason Bay show at Outbound Squad. We're done playing small. We're building a team. And we're ready to make a big impact in H2.

LFG

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 day ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Adam Block is the former CRO at Motive, a sales org with 3,000+ reps. Here's how they successfully moved up-market to grow their enterprise segment...

We recorded this right before Adam announced his departure from Motive.

Three things he said hit hard. And they contradict the instinct of most sales leaders I talk to.


✅ 1. Cut book sizes. Always.

Adam: "I don't recall a time that I've ever reduced book sizes and didn't see productivity go up."

The instinct as a rep, and as a sales leader looking at TAM, is more accounts equals more opportunity. The opposite is true at enterprise. 

Sometimes it makes sense for a strategic AE to have 1-3 accounts. With entire teams supporting them. 

If your enterprise AEs are still carrying 50+ accounts, try cutting their books by a quarter to a half. Watch what happens.

As a rep, disqualify a big chunk of your accounts. 


✅ 2. Kill the three-pod model.

Motive used to run new logo, expansion sales, and CSM as three separate roles. The classic pod. They collapsed it.

Why: too many cooks, no single owner with a net number...etc.

The fix. One account manager owns the customer forever (or one new-logo AE keeps the account on the largest deals). They become the quarterback. They pull in product, support, finance, or supply chain when needed. But there's one name on the account.

Result: better retention AND more expansion revenue. Customers stopped getting passed around.

If you're running the three-pod model and your expansion number isn't there, this might be your fix.


✅ 3. The 90-second test.

Adam was a part of every new hire class. His ask before any rep touched a single account: 

Explain who Motive is, what we do, how we do it better, the impact, and where we've done it. In 90 to 120 seconds. To your parent. To a neighbor. To anyone.

If a new SDR or AE can't do that, they can't have a real conversation with a buyer. Period.


~~~


If you're org is moving up-market, or struggling to do so, you'll love this episode. 

We talked about:

1) Adam's journey from VP of Sales to CRO at Motive
2) How Motive moved from down-market to enterprise in 3 years
3) Proven tactics for moving up-market

Watch the full interview here: 
https://lnkd.in/gRBCbpFw

author.name: Jason Bay
author.info: Turn strangers into customers | Outbound Coach, Trainer, and SKO Speaker for B2B sales teams

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
We recently helped 300+ AEs at iHeartMedia increase self-sourced meetings by 40%+

And they were not starting at zero.

H2 pipeline is alarmingly thin for many sales orgs right now.

And you might be getting a lot of resistance from AEs on doing outbound:

"This is marketing's job."
"This is our BDR's job."
"I don't have time for PG, I'm working deals."

But let's face it: the whole "let's cut AI agents loose to find us leads" hasn't been working for most sales orgs. 

Blame it on the org. Blame it on the tool. I don't care.

The need for AEs to self-source pipeline isn't going away.

Self-sourcing is an ATTITUDE. It either exists in your sales culture or it doesn't.

Here's what we're seeing working right now:


✅ 1) Train the AI

Signing your team up for Claude accounts isn't good enough. You need examples of great outbound and process loaded into your LLM as skills. You have to train the AI for it to work well. And you need a ton of examples of what good looks like. 


✅ 2) Up-skill the AEs as much as you do the BDRs

Train on what's working in 2026. Spend time on the phones, email, LinkedIn, etc. 

AEs need role-playing practice, weekly coaching, etc.


✅ 3) Enable front-line leaders

Too often, I see groups of front-line managers who don't know how to outbound. How could you possibly expect an AE to prioritize PG when you don't know how to teach it? Enable front-line leaders on how to incorporate PG into 1on1s, how to develop/coach reps, and how to outbound themselves.


✅ 4) Rub shoulders. 

Front-line managers get in the pit with their AEs every week. Make calls together into tier-1 accounts and their toughest-to-reach prospects.


✅ 5) Arm AEs with the right tools

Too often, I see a sales org with BDRs using a sales engagement platform, but the AEs have to do outbound manually with zero tooling. Give AEs the same access to sales engagement tools as you do for your SDRs & BDRs. You have ZERO accountability if you can’t measure outbound activity. We love Orum 🥇's* dialing solution.


✅ 6) Top-down involvement

CRO and VP of Sales should participate in outbound efforts by giving public shout-outs in Slack for reps landing meetings through outbound. They should celebrate effort, share stories, and get the team pumped up.


✅ 7) Data

Get best-in-class email and phone data. We love TitanX* for that. You can’t expect AEs to make dials without access to solid email addresses & mobile numbers.


~~~


Creating a PG culture with AEs doesn't happen overnight. But hitting your number in 2026/27 won't be possible without it. Start now.

What would you add to this list?


*Our partners, not sponsors

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
"I send 10,000+ cold emails a day, but I have to say: NOBODY should cold call."

I could not believe my eyes. Had to comment on this post.

🍀Ian Hill's response really hit me..."Have we already come full circle that we're just spray and praying again?"

He nailed it. At the end of the day, all this "fully automate outbound with this Claude workflow" nonsense is exactly that. 

It's a re-packaged version of spraying and praying. It's incrementally "better" than dropping the same message into a sequencer and sending it. But not by much. 

- It's still noise
- If you're sending thousands per day, the quality isn't great
- It p*sses off our buyers

It's the equivalent of littering in the park and not giving a f*ck about how it impacts other people.

I'm 100% bullish on the phones and in-person right now because it's the least crowded way to get attention. 

Execs get maybe a dozen cold calls per day if they're bigwigs. No one's reaching out to meet them in person. Nobody's stopping by their office.

Please don't fall for this nonsense.

Pick up the phone and get after it.

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
If I was a BDR or AE trying to break into my dream account, here's exactly what I'd do:

I'd come up with an offer that made the buyer feel IRRESPONSIBLE for not taking the meeting with me.

And I'd do this with a killer offer. 

This is the outbound datapoint everyone should be building their 2026/27 strategy around. Offers increase email reply rates by up to 28% (Gong)

Offers are NOT:

• A free demo
• A gift card
• A “meet and greet”

They're tangible gives. Like a cart audit let's say if you sell an ecommerce solution. Dig through the checkout process and analyze:

• Missed upsell opportunities
• Broken processes
• Lack of cart abandon follow up
etc

Here's how you can use offers to increase the effectiveness of your cold outreach:



✅ Offer #1 - Pitch The Blind Date

Position who the buyer will be meeting with. I'd hype up the AE, sales engineer, or myself depending on my industry experience. 

Example: A client of ours sells an automated welding solution

Example email CTA: 

"Our welding solutions engineer, Paul, is working with Hitch manufacturers like CURT to help overcome the welder shortage and produce consistent, high-quality welds.

Interested in a quick intro?"


✅ Offer #2 - 1:Many Offers

High-quality, reusable insights that feel tailored. Competitive benchmarks, industry research, or best practice guides.

Example: We have a client that sells to a clinical ops solution. They surveyed dozens of clients in key areas around clinical trials.

Example email CTA: 

"We ran a benchmarking analysis with a few of your peers like Ultromics and MEDRhythms to find avg. study start times, drop-out rates, insights on how they shorten build times.

Interested in seeing how you stack up?"


✅ Offer #3 - 1:1 Offers

These are custom-tailored experiences or resources created specifically for the prospect. This works best at the enterprise & strategic levels.

Examples:

- A cyber risk analysis
- A benchmarking analysis
- A workshop
- A personalized audit of a website checkout flow.
- Visiting and experiencing the brand firsthand, then sharing insights.
- Offering free data, licenses, or pilots.

Example: An EdTech rep would use language like this when reaching out to school districts

"We compiled a report of how WCUS stacks up against the top schools in WA & OR.

Free next week to run through the report together?

Tom

P.S. If nothing else, you’ll see how other PNW school districts are minimizing student inequity and achievement gaps to increase student readiness and graduation rates"


~~~


Offers are the future of outbound. It'll help your emails, calls, and DMs cut through the noise.

What are you giving the buyer, that even if they decided not to buy your solution, makes the time they spend with you worthwhile?

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
If I were looking to hire my next all-star sales rep, here's exactly what I'd focus on...

We've worked with 250+ sales teams and over 10,000 reps in the last 5-6 years. Teams as large as Shopify, Zoom, Rippling, etc. And teams with 3-5 reps rolling out an outbound motion for the first time.
20,000+ reps.

The best reps fit into one very specific archetype:

1. The grinder
2. The procrastinator
3. The cyborg
4. The owner

A grinder never misses their activity target. They muscle their way through pipegen. They're in the top 25%, but never the top 10%.

The procrastinator only builds pipeline at the beginning of every quarter...when they don't have any. They're last.

The cyborg leverages every AI tool imaginable. They're a modern day version of the grinder, but they never develop a BIG book of business because their buyers don't respond to AI-written emails. And they don't like to pick up the phone.

The best archetype we've seen is the OWNER.

This rep acts as a CEO of their territory.

They:

✅ 1) Disqualify. Ruthlessly disqualify opportunities unworthy of their energy, time, and attention.

✅ 2) Offer. Consultative and make strategic “gives” to buyers early and often to earn their time.

✅ 3) Provoke. Engage buyers with a strong point of view on their problem that compels action now.

And they book 4.3x more meetings than their avg. performing peers.

They know that in a world full of AI, they must be Too Good To Ignore.

The owner is the archetype you should be hiring for in your next best rep.

Agree or disagree?

author.name: Jason Bay

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
"Cold outbound" is only ONE flavor of "pipegen." It's the coldest, least effective way to engage buyers.

I'm not saying it doesn't work. We've built out our entire business at Outbound Squad by helping companies like Shopify, Rippling, Zoom, etc crush at it.

But it's the very last thing you should do.

Mitch Thomas runs enablement at Brooksource for 180+ large enterprise AEs with ZERO BDR support.

They self-source all of their pipeline. 

Do they make cold calls? Yes. 

But it's not the only way their very best reps source pipeline:

1) Referrals
2) Community events & philanthropy
3) Industry associations
4) Tradeshows & conferences
5) Following champions to new businesses
6) Asking for introductions
7) In-person drop bys

Phone, email, and LinkedIn are just tools. You can choose to have ice cold conversations, or you can "warm them up" by looking for a better path in.

When we worked with Brooksource, they were already converting 22% of their outbound calls into qualified meetings. 

After we worked together, it was 35%+. I've never seen it higher with any other client.

I'd love to take more credit for it, but a big part was calling into leads that weren't completely cold. 

If there's one way to virtually guarantee that you land more meetings, it's do just that. 

In this interview, we talked about:

- How Brooksource’s 180+ AEs self-source pipeline without BDRs
- Building a world-class onboarding process
- How enablement builds strong partnership with sales

Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eTuQxV4T

author.name: Jason Bay
author.info: Turn strangers into customers | Outbound Coach, Trainer, and SKO Speaker for B2B sales teams

postedAt.postedAgoText: 1 week ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
250+ sales teams. 10,000+ reps. Here’s the best cold call opener…

👇

We’ve studied hundreds of thousands of cold calls across 250+ sales teams in the last 6 years. 

And there’s one thing we can tell you for sure:

The best reps all use different openers. That’s right. There’s no one opener. Or handful of openers that work the best.

BUT…here’s what every great opener that led to a meeting had in common.

The opener has three parts:


✅ Part 1) A greeting

“Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…”

They answer the first question every prospect has: who are you?


✅ Part 2) The three Ts

Here’s where it starts to get interesting. Each of the openers leverages one of what we call the “three Ts”:

Trigger: Make it timely by finding signals/triggers
Tension: Make it relevant by sharing a problem
Trust: Make it credible by leveraging social proof

Sometimes they include all three. But every time, they include at least one.

“Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…saw you’re hiring welders…”

That’s an example of leveraging a trigger.


✅ Part 3) Permission

Gong data from 300k+ cold calls supports the same. Permission-based openers outperform any other opener by nearly 5x.

“Hi [first name], it’s [your first name…saw you’re hiring welders…have a minute for me to share why I called you specifically?”

A simple permission-based question.


~~~


Now here’s what it looks like when you start to get creative:

Greeting + Tension + Permission: “Hi Dave, was calling about the welder shortage everyone’s dealing with right now. Got a minute for me to share more?”

Greeting + Trigger + Tension + Trust + Permission: “Hi Kate, noticed you’re hiring MIG welders, not sure if you’re running into challenges automating some of those low volume welds folks like Caterpillar have had. Got a min? Promise to be brief.”

Greeting + Trust + Permission: “Hi Tom, we work with several trailer hitch manufacturers like XYZ. It’s Jason with ABC COMPANY. Heard our name tossed around?” (h/t to Armand Farrokh)


~~~


The best cold call openers are the ones you create. Trigger, tension, trust is just the ingredients. You make your own recipe.

Want more? 

I was just featured in HubSpot’s most recent The Science of Scaling newsletter. 

Check it out here: https://lnkd.in/gFHWKk6p

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Not all humans are fit for outbound

Tara DiCristo-Schmitt is the CRO at Houzz. She also spent 5 years as a VP of Sales at HubSpot and started her sales career at ADP.

Tara has a hot take I can't stop thinking about (and couldn’t agree with more).

Stop trying to convert your inbound team to an outbound team.

This is the request we get more than any other. "We've been an inbound shop. Pipeline's down. We need to flip the team."

Keep your inbound team intact. Or in the AI era, reduce headcount. That's revenue you can depend on. Don't break the thing that works.

For outbound, hire net new. Build the army separately, with people who actually want to do the work.

You can teach skill. You cannot teach will. A generation of reps joined inbound shops because they were promised they wouldn't have to cold call. You can run the best enablement program in the world, and most of them still won't cut it.

Every "we tried mandatory call blocks and it didn't work" story starts with a team hired for one motion and asked to do another.

In this episode, we talked about:

✅ Tara's journey from ADP rep (and one of the first women sales leaders) to HubSpot to CRO at Houzz

✅ Why retraining inbound reps to outbound is a trap

✅ The director days ritual that exposes coaching gaps

✅ Her weekly rhythm as a CRO

And much, much more

Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/eUyC3nXd

author.name: Jason Bay
author.info: Turn strangers into customers | Outbound Coach, Trainer, and SKO Speaker for B2B sales teams

postedAt.postedAgoText: 2 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
I disagree that the first line of a cold email is to get them to read the second line.

That's not how most people actually read written content.

Google, Nielsen, CMSWire, EyeQuant...they've all done studies over the years. 

And the conclusion is this:

- People read in either an F shape
- OR people read just the left-hand side of the page
- 2/3 of people skip the intro and jump straight to the end
- The middle body is the most overlooked part of the content

When I closely pay attention to my own habits, I open the email, read the first line, then immediately skip to the CTA to see what the ask is.

So what's the objective of the first line?

Two parts:

1) Drive the open
2) Build urgency

The first line of the email shows up in the preview text before the prospect ever opens it. To drive opens, it's just as important as the subject line.

Next, the first line should build urgency by talking about something hyper-relevant to the buyer. Something going on with their business, a challenge across their industry, a relevant competitor, etc

Here are three options, using an HR solution as an example:

✅ Trigger opener

Open with a trigger that helps answer the "why now?" question for the buyer.

"Saw you just opened locations in Italy and France..."


✅ Tension

Open with a problem you suspect they might have, or that's prevalent across their industry.

"International hiring can create a ton of compliance challenges that slows down hiring."


✅ Trust

Open with a social proof statement. 

"Not sure if you're running into this, but ABC COMPANY ran into tons of compliance challenges that slowed down hiring when they went international for the first time."


~~~


Let's not kid ourselves. Buyers are rarely reading our entire email the first time around. 

Give them a reason to open. 

Then give them a reason to pay attention.

Leverage triggers, tension statements, and trust statements.

author.info: Turn strangers into customers | Outbound Coach, Trainer, and SKO Speaker for B2B sales teams

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

content:
Rippling has some crazy good plays for booking meetings with enterprise HR leaders.

Ken Amar runs the enterprise SDR org at Rippling. Before that, he was SDR of the Year at Outreach.

Ken's whole career has been based on one bet: when everyone zigs, you zag.

Here are three outbound plays his team is running right now to book quality enterprise meetings.


✅ 1. The groundswell play.

Ken's reps don't open enterprise accounts by calling the CHRO. They call the HR analyst three levels down, offer a $50 gift card for 30 minutes of their time, and run a mini discovery call to learn the tech stack, the renewal date, and what's frustrating people day to day.

Then they ask the analyst to multi-thread them up to their director.

Connect-to-meeting rate on a normal enterprise cold call: about 5%. Multi-thread rate on the groundswell play: 50%. Five times more effective. And a $50 gift card to a Fortune 1000 CHRO is in poor taste anyway. They can afford their own lunch.


✅ 2. The Broadway ticket play.

One of Ken's reps researched a CHRO in Denver. Microbiology major. Broadway fan. They sent her a leather notebook, two Broadway tickets, and a handwritten note referencing her upcoming renewal with a competitor.

Two weeks later, she emailed back out of the blue. That gift generated a multimillion-dollar opportunity.

There are 100 other SDRs trying to reach that same person with the same automated sequence. Be different.


✅ 3. The suite invite as the email lead.

Rippling rents NBA suites in cities where they have high-value CHROs. The whole cadence is built around the invite. Only 11 seats.

Open rate: 70%. Reply rate: 41%. Even the no's are warm.

This lines up with the Gong data we cite all the time. Offers increase reply rate by 28%. Direct meeting asks decrease reply rate by 44%. Trade your asks for offers.


~~~


The full episode goes deeper on two more plays his team uses (ghostwritten exec emails and custom landing pages at scale), Ken's origin story at Outreach, and his hot take on why AI SDRs are just the next SEP.

In this episode, we talked about:

- Ken's journey from rep to Sr. Director at Rippling
- Unconventional plays that book qualified meetings
- The future of AI in sales

Watch the full interview here: https://lnkd.in/gMZkkqkx

content:
The buyer needs to RESPECT you before they can like and trust you.

Damn, let that one sink in. 

Paul Salamanca shared that in our recent podcast episode. 

He has over a decade of experience as a VP of Sales. And his small but mighty BDR team at Cymon AI is crushing it right now. Two BDRs book 30-40 enterprise meetings per month between the two of them. Yes, per month.

We can do many things, unintentionally, to lower our status with buyers. Behaviors that immediately signal that we're not a peer.

Like saying "sorry" for cold calling.

Or being overly grateful to the buyer for taking time to meet with you. 

Or never pushing back on their requests, or thinking about gives/gets.

We can level the playing field by being true experts at what we sell, speaking to the buyer like a peer, and setting boundaries when needed. 

Paul and I had a killer chat about that and much more:

- Paul's journey from rep to VP of Sales to founder
- Pipegen techniques working to book a ton of qualified meetings
- How his SDRs book 15-20+ enterprise meetings each per month
- The future of AI in sales

postedAt.postedAgoText: 3 weeks ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn
